title: PA State Rep.SherylDelozierServing Pennsylvania's 88th Legislative District
raw_text:
Jun
30
Press Release
Delozier: House Passes Resolution Designating June as Scoliosis Awareness Month in Pennsylvania
Read More

ai_summary.summary: The Pennsylvania House of Representatives passed a resolution designating June as Scoliosis Awareness Month in the state. Representative Delozier supported or sponsored this ceremonial resolution to raise awareness about scoliosis, a spinal condition. The resolution does not create new law or funding.

title: Update Election Integrity Voter ID
raw_text:
Update on Election Integrity, Voter ID
Public support for measures that enhance election integrity is overwhelmingly strong. A Franklin & Marshall College poll from 2021 revealed that 74% of Pennsylvanians, which encompass Democrats and Independents as well as Republicans, support voter ID laws. Despite this bipartisan support, partisan operating rules have halted progress on voter ID, preventing Pennsylvania voters from having the final say on the matter.
A voter identification constitutional amendment,
House Bill 891
, has languished in the House State Government Committee for more than a year, despite bipartisan support. House Republicans launched a discharge petition in an attempt to advance the measure, but it has not yet moved.
In addition to working toward voter ID, here are some other election integrity initiatives that have been put forward:
вАҐ
House Bill 1263
would require a county board of elections, prior to the certification of the results of an election, to conduct a manual audit or an automated and independent audit of the voting systems used in randomly selected precincts.
вАҐ
House Bill 1264
would require the auditor general to conduct an annual performance audit of the county results of each election to ensure the integrity of PAвАЩs electoral process.
вАҐ
House Bill 773
would encourage candidates to utilize online filing for campaign finance reports, which would increase public transparency while saving the taxpayers money.
вАҐ
House Bill 978
would create an election complaint procedure and would provide statutory structure to ensure all election complaints are reviewed and responded to.
вАҐ
House Bill 1420
would protect voting rights through three broad concepts: increased access, increased security and modernization.
вАҐ
House Bill 1883
would improve the accuracy and integrity of voter registration records by cross-referencing them with jury duty lists.
вАҐ
House Bill 2053
would expand and formalize requirements for the certification of any voting machine by the secretary of State, as well as for additional pre-election machine testing in all counties.
вАҐ
House Bill 2054
would strengthen PAвАЩs requirements for voting machine manufacturers to resolve any defects or failures occurring in their machines.
вАҐ
House Bill 2144
would prohibit artificially generated voices from being used for political campaign purposes.
вАҐ
House Bill 2542
would require the Department of StateвАЩs election return website to include the number of precincts reporting and the total number of precincts in its preliminary election results in each race.
Election integrity is fundamental for the proper functioning of democracy. By implementing robust verification processes, addressing system failures and fostering community engagement, we can uphold public trust in our institutions.

ai_summary.summary: A Pennsylvania legislator issued a statement listing multiple election-related bills that have been introduced in the state House, including a proposed constitutional amendment requiring voter ID that has been stalled in committee for over a year. The release describes the status of approximately ten other election administration bills covering topics such as audits, voting machine certification, campaign finance filing, and voter registration accuracy. No new legislation was passed or signed; this is an informational update on pending proposals.

title: Local Government Needs
raw_text:
Local Government Needs in the 88th District: Borough and Township Vacancies
Mechanicsburg Borough
Currently, Mechanicsburg Borough does not have any open seats on boards or commissions.
New Cumberland Borough
Currently, New Cumberland Borough does not have any open seats on boards or commissions.
Shiremanstown Borough
Residents are encouraged to check for updates if they are interested in serving on a board or commission. Open positions are currently being confirmed.
Hampden Township
Hampden Township continues to seek volunteers.
Openings exist on the following boards, commissions and committees:
вАҐ	Golf Advisory Board
вАҐ	Civil Service Commission
вАҐ	Industrial Development Authority
вАҐ	Planning Commission
вАҐ	Zoning Hearing Board
вАҐ	Good Hope Ad Hoc Committee
Residents who would like to be considered can apply through the
townshipвАЩs volunteer portal
.
Hampden Township also keeps applications on file for future openings, ensuring interested residents are considered as vacancies arise.
Lower Allen Township
Openings exist on the following commissions and committees:
вАҐ	Civil Service Commission
вАҐ	Planning Commission
вАҐ	Pension Committee
The township welcomes new volunteers year round. Due to limited openings and long appointment terms, positions may not become available immediately, but submitting interest helps the township identify future candidates.
Residents can apply by sending a resume and cover letter outlining qualifications to Township Manager Rebecca Davis at the Municipal Services Center, 2233 Gettysburg Road, Camp Hill, PA 17011, or by emailing rdavis@latwp.org.
More information about boards and commissions is available on the township website at
latwp.org
.

title: Delozier: House Passes Resolution Designating June as Scoliosis Awareness Month in Pennsylvania
raw_text:
Delozier: House Passes Resolution Designating June as Scoliosis Awareness Month in Pennsylvania
June 30, 2026
HARRISBURG вАФ The Pennsylvania House of Representatives today passed
House Resolution 531
, introduced by Rep. Sheryl M. Delozier (RвАУCumberland), officially designating June 2026 as Scoliosis Awareness Month in the Commonwealth.
Scoliosis is a chronic spinal condition affecting one in 40 children and up to one in three adults. The disease, often developing during periods of rapid growth between ages 10 and 15, is idiopathic in 80% of cases, meaning the cause is unknown.
Delozier highlighted the story of Ella, a constituent from Hampden Township in the 88th District, whose diagnosis of severe idiopathic scoliosis in middle school illustrates the importance of early screening.
вАЬBy recognizing June as Scoliosis Awareness Month, we are shining a light on a condition that too often goes undiagnosed,вАЭ said Delozier. вАЬEarly detection can change the course of someoneвАЩs life, and EllaвАЩs story is a powerful reminder of that. Her courage and advocacy inspire all of us to do more for the thousands of Pennsylvanians living with scoliosis.
вАЬIвАЩm proud to elevate her voice and the voices of so many others who face scoliosis with strength. With this resolution, we are reaffirming our commitment to education, support and better outcomes for individuals across Pennsylvania.вАЭ
After her pediatrician identified a rib hump at a routine well visit, Ella was diagnosed with a spinal curve of approximately 50 degrees and began bracing for more than 20 hours each day.
When bracing proved insufficient, she underwent spinal fusion surgery at the ChildrenвАЩs Hospital of Philadelphia, successfully reducing her curve from nearly 60 degrees to under 15 degrees.
With the passage of House Resolution 531, Pennsylvania reaffirms its commitment to promoting early detection and supporting individuals living with scoliosis.
For questions about this resolution, please call DelozierвАЩs office at 717-761-4665.
Representative Sheryl Delozier
88th District
Pennsylvania House of Representatives
Media Contact: Katelin Morrison
717.260.6502
Kmorrison@pahousegop.com
RepDelozier.com / Facebook.com/RepDelozier
Share

The resolution is symbolic and does not create law, allocate funding, or mandate any specific programs. Delozier used the occasion to highlight a constituent's personal experience with scoliosis to underscore the importance of early screening.

title: Delozier: House Passes Bipartisan Bill to Fund Municipal Bridge Improvements
House Passes Bipartisan Bill to Allow Reinstatement of Parental Rights, Says Delozier
Continue Reading
raw_text:
House Passes Bipartisan Bill to Allow Reinstatement of Parental Rights, Says Delozier
June 23, 2026
HARRISBURG вАУ Legislation allowing courts to reinstate parental rights has passed the House with bipartisan support, announced Rep. Sheryl M. Delozier (R Cumberland). Delozier, having a longstanding history of advocating for our children and second chances, collaborated with Rep. Rick Krajewski (D-Philadelphia) on this bipartisan initiative.
Pennsylvania is one of the few remaining states without a mechanism to restore parental rights, even when a parent has demonstrated rehabilitation and when adoption is no longer an option for the child.
House Bill 133
would create a legal process permitting reinstatement only when a child has not been adopted, when adoption is no longer a viable option, and when a court determines that reunification will support the childвАЩs safety, stability and well being.
вАЬRep. Krajewski and I worked together from the beginning to craft a bill that puts children first,вАЭ Delozier said. вАЬThis bipartisan vote shows that no matter our district or political party, we agree that children deserve every opportunity for stability, permanency and meaningful family connections.вАЭ
Under the bill, petitions for reinstatement would be permitted only if:
вАҐ	At least 15 months have passed since the termination of parental rights, or the youth is at least 17 years old.
вАҐ	The child has not been adopted, has no pending adoption petition or an adoptive placement has ended.
вАҐ	Reinstatement aligns with the childвАЩs best interests and is supported by evidence presented to the court.
вАЬThis bipartisan vote reflects a shared understanding across the aisle: second chances matter,вАЭ Delozier added. вАЬThrough initiatives like Clean Slate, IвАЩve seen firsthand that Pennsylvanians can transform their lives. House Bill 133 ensures that our laws recognize those transformations when a child stands to benefit. I am hopeful this bill will continue to garner bipartisan support as it advances through the legislative process.вАЭ
Child welfare professionals, attorneys, and community advocates testified in support of the measure during a November hearing, emphasizing that reinstatement would not be automatic. Instead, it would serve as a deliberate, child centered remedy when a parentвАЩs growth and sustained rehabilitation make reunification both safe and appropriate.
The bill now heads to the Senate for consideration.
Representative Sheryl Delozier
88th District
Pennsylvania House of Representatives
Media Contact: Katelin Morrison
717.260.6502
Kmorrison@pahousegop.com
RepDelozier.com / Facebook.com/RepDelozier

ai_summary.summary: The Pennsylvania House passed House Bill 133, co-sponsored by Rep. Delozier and Rep. Krajewski, which would create a legal process allowing courts to reinstate parental rights that were previously terminated. The bill applies only in cases where a child has not been adopted and adoption is no longer a viable option, and requires a court to find that reunification serves the child's best interests. The bill now moves to the Pennsylvania Senate for consideration.

title: Delozier: Budget Compromise Invests in Students, Law Enforcement Without Draining State Savings
raw_text:
Delozier: Budget Compromise Invests in Students, Law Enforcement Without Draining State Savings
July 12, 2026
HARRISBURG вАУ Citing sound investments that benefit Cumberland County residents, Rep. Sheryl M. Delozier (R-Cumberland) supported the 2026-27 state budget adopted by the Pennsylvania General Assembly today. Following final passage, she issued this statement:
вАЬToday, I voted in favor of a budget proposal that is a compromise between the House and Senate that strengthens investments in education, supports first responders and increases funding for domestic violence and senior services. These are areas of importance to Cumberland County and residents of the 88th District, where our communities continue to grow and thrive.
вАЬThe agreed-to budget provides historic levels of funding for education. As a result, every school in the Commonwealth will be well-funded to teach our future leaders. There will be increases due to the adequacy gap formula changes that I advocated for last budget cycle for our local school districts, including Cumberland Valley, West Shore and Mechanicsburg Area.
вАЬThis budget funds services for our seniors to stay in their homes, funds work for victims of domestic violence, and the Victim Act provides dollars for training and solving cold cases to protect our communities.
вАЬPerhaps most importantly, the budget compromise accomplishes these goals without raising taxes or touching the Rainy Day Fund, a state savings account designed to provide support during economic downturns. It earns money as the state invests in our future.
вАЬAs a compromise, there is spending in the budget I don't agree with. $50.8 billion is higher than I had hoped the spending would be, but this budget works to achieve important goals that do not jeopardize the long-term financial health of our Commonwealth. In last yearвАЩs budget, House Republicans championed major policy changes that will allow for more economic growth. Time will allow us to reap the benefits of those policies and lead to more flexible budgeting in future cycles.
вАЬLooking ahead, our communities are positioned for continued growth and stability, ensuring we can meet todayвАЩs needs while preparing responsibly for tomorrow. It is incumbent on the state legislature to work for a compromise to get a budget passed to allow our state and local governments to continue the important services provided to our constituents.вАЭ
Representative Sheryl Delozier
88th District
Pennsylvania House of Representatives
Share

ai_summary.summary: Rep. Delozier voted in favor of Pennsylvania's 2026-27 state budget, a $50.8 billion spending plan that increases education funding, funding for senior services, domestic violence programs, and law enforcement cold case work. She issued a statement explaining her vote and noting the budget does not raise taxes or draw from the state's Rainy Day Fund. She acknowledged the total spending was higher than she preferred but voted yes as a compromise.
